content.text: Emergence in language models refers to capabilities that appear abruptly at a certain scale — abilities that are effectively absent in smaller models and reliably present in larger ones, without those capabilities having been explicitly trained. Wei et al. (2022) systematically documented over 100 such emergent abilities across a range of benchmarks. The phenomenon is striking because it is not predicted by smooth extrapolation of smaller-model performance: below a threshold, accuracy is near-random; above it, accuracy jumps sharply. Understanding emergence matters because it determines what you can expect from a model of a given size — and where adding more compute stops helping.
What Counts as Emergent

A capability is emergent if it satisfies two conditions: (1) it is not present in smaller models at the same family, and (2) the transition is sharp rather than a smooth power-law improvement. Documented examples include:
Capability
Approximate emergence threshold
Benchmark
3-digit arithmetic
~13B parameters
BIG-Bench arithmetic
Word-in-context disambiguation
~50B parameters
WiC
Chain-of-thought reasoning
~100B parameters
GSM8K, math word problems
Multi-step logical reasoning
~100B parameters
BIG-Bench logical deduction
Few-shot translation (unseen language pairs)
~7B parameters
WMT, FLORES
Measuring Emergence

To characterise emergence on a task, you evaluate the same model family at multiple scales and plot accuracy against model size (or compute). The signature of emergence is an inflection — a region where the curve transitions from flat (near-random) to rising sharply:

import numpy as np

# Hypothetical accuracy vs. model size data for a multi-step reasoning task
# (param counts in billions, accuracy in [0, 1])
model_sizes_b = [0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 7, 13, 30, 70, 130, 175]
accuracies    = [0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.09, 0.11, 0.15, 0.45, 0.72, 0.81, 0.85]


def detect_emergence_threshold(
    sizes: list[float],
    accs:  list[float],
    baseline: float = 0.20,     # random-ish performance below threshold
    jump:     float = 0.20,     # must exceed baseline + jump to count as emergent
) -> float | None:
    """
    Find the smallest model size at which accuracy first crosses (baseline + jump).
    Returns the size in billions, or None if no emergence is detected.
    """
    threshold = baseline + jump
    for size, acc in zip(sizes, accs):
        if acc >= threshold:
            return size
    return None


threshold = detect_emergence_threshold(model_sizes_b, accuracies)
print(f"Emergence detected at ~{threshold}B parameters")  # ~30B


def compute_sharpness(sizes, accs, window: int = 2) -> list[tuple]:
    """
    Estimate local slope of accuracy vs. log(size) over a sliding window.
    High slope regions indicate the emergence transition.
    """
    log_sizes = np.log10(sizes)
    slopes = []
    for i in range(len(sizes) - window):
        d_acc  = accs[i + window] - accs[i]
        d_log  = log_sizes[i + window] - log_sizes[i]
        slopes.append((sizes[i], d_acc / (d_log + 1e-9)))
    return slopes


for size, slope in compute_sharpness(model_sizes_b, accuracies):
    print(f"{size:6.1f}B params → slope {slope:.3f}")
Why Does Emergence Happen?

The leading hypothesis is that emergent capabilities are multi-component: each component scales smoothly with model size, but the capability only manifests when all components are present simultaneously. This creates a threshold effect even when no single component has one. Consider multi-step arithmetic: the model needs to (1) parse the problem, (2) recall arithmetic facts, (3) apply them in sequence, (4) carry intermediate results. If each component is present with probability p(N) that grows with model size N, the joint probability p(N)^k rises steeply above a threshold where p(N) crosses a critical value.

For k = 5 components each at p = 0.8, joint success is 0.33. At p = 0.95, joint success is 0.77. A small improvement in each component produces a large improvement in the overall task — the curve looks discontinuous even though the underlying components scaled smoothly.
The Metric Artefact Debate

Schaeffer et al. (2023) argued that many observed emergence curves are artefacts of the choice of metric. Non-linear metrics (exact-match accuracy, pass@1 on code) amplify small continuous improvements into apparent phase transitions. When the same data is re-plotted with continuous metrics (token-level accuracy, Brier score, log-probability), the curve is smooth across scales.

def exact_match_accuracy(predictions: list[str], references: list[str]) -> float:
    """
    Binary exact-match accuracy: 1 if prediction == reference, else 0.
    Non-linear — small improvement in token probability can flip 0 → 1
    on a multi-step problem, producing a sharp-looking emergence curve.
    """
    return sum(p == r for p, r in zip(predictions, references)) / len(references)


def token_log_probability(log_probs_per_token: list[float]) -> float:
    """
    Mean token log-probability: a continuous, linear metric.
    When plotted against scale, this typically shows a smooth improvement
    rather than an apparent phase transition.
    """
    return sum(log_probs_per_token) / len(log_probs_per_token)


# Schaeffer et al.'s point: the same underlying improvement looks
# 'emergent' under exact_match_accuracy but smooth under token_log_probability.
# Neither view is wrong — they measure different things. The practical
# question is which metric matches your deployment criterion.
Practical Implications
Model selection.

For tasks known to require multi-step reasoning (math, code, planning), small models may perform at chance regardless of prompting strategy. The threshold is around 7–13B parameters for most such tasks.
Benchmark evaluation.
Evaluating only at one scale can be misleading. If your model is near the emergence threshold, a 2× scale increase may produce a large capability jump — or nothing, if you are still below it.
Safety unpredictability.
Emergent capabilities include undesired ones — deceptive reasoning, jailbreak susceptibility, persuasion. A model that appears safe at small scale may exhibit new failure modes after a scale jump.
Chain-of-thought unlocks emergence cheaply.
For many reasoning tasks, CoT prompting lowers the effective emergence threshold — a 7B model with CoT can match a 70B model without it on some benchmarks.

Emergence is best understood not as magic but as a measurement phenomenon: tasks that require several sub-skills to all be correct simultaneously look discontinuous even when each sub-skill improves continuously. What changes at scale is not the kind of learning but the reliability of each component — and reliability compounds.
Why It Matters

Emergence is central to both the opportunities and risks of large language models. On the opportunity side, emergent capabilities explain why scaling a model from 7B to 70B parameters can suddenly enable reliable multi-step reasoning, code generation, or translation into unseen language pairs — none of which were explicitly trained. This means the return on compute investment is non-linear: below the emergence threshold, additional compute produces minimal capability gain; above it, new task categories become available without changing anything about training.

On the risk side, emergence is unpredictable in advance. Safety evaluations performed on a 7B model may not predict the behaviour of a 70B model if dangerous capabilities (deceptive reasoning, persuasion at scale, jailbreak susceptibility) emerge between those sizes. This makes safety-by-evaluation challenging: you cannot observe all capabilities at training time and must actively probe for emergent behaviours across scale. Regulatory frameworks increasingly require this kind of systematic capability elicitation.
Why Emergence Is Useful to Understand
Predicts which tasks require larger models.
Tasks that involve multi-step composition of sub-skills (mathematical reasoning, multi-hop QA) are candidates for emergence. Knowing this helps avoid wasting compute on models too small for the task.
Motivates chain-of-thought prompting.

CoT effectively lowers the emergence threshold by decomposing multi-step tasks into sequential steps the model can handle individually, enabling smaller models to exhibit capabilities otherwise requiring 10x more parameters.
Informs evaluation strategy.

Understanding that binary metrics amplify smooth improvements into apparent phase transitions (Schaeffer et al. 2023) guides evaluation design: use continuous metrics alongside binary ones to distinguish genuine emergence from measurement artefacts.
Guides compute allocation.
If your task has a known emergence threshold (~13B, ~70B, ~100B parameters), you can target training runs above that threshold rather than scaling incrementally through capability-flat regions.
Trade-offs and Controversies

The definition of emergence is contested. Schaeffer et al. (2023) showed that many emergence curves disappear when continuous metrics replace binary ones. Under token-level log-probability, performance on tasks previously labelled "emergent" improves smoothly with scale — the apparent discontinuity was an artefact of the metric, not a real phase transition. This implies that "emergence" in LLMs may not be fundamentally different from ordinary scaling — just harder to observe with coarse metrics.

Counterarguments exist: some capabilities genuinely appear only above threshold because they require all components to work simultaneously (the multi-component hypothesis). But the debate highlights a practical pitfall: researchers who label a capability as "emergent" based solely on a binary accuracy curve may be measuring their evaluation metric's non-linearity, not a real capability threshold. Interpretation requires multi-metric analysis.
Emergence is unpredictable before the fact.

There is no reliable method to predict at what scale a specific new capability will emerge. This makes planning model deployments difficult — a 10x scale increase may or may not unlock the capability you need.
Safety evaluation gap.

If dangerous capabilities emerge after safety evaluation is complete, evaluations conducted on smaller checkpoints provide false assurance. Continuous monitoring across training checkpoints is necessary but expensive.
When Emergence Is a Practical Concern

Emergence is practically important when selecting a model for a task that requires multi-step reasoning. If you are evaluating a 7B model and getting near-random performance on a GSM8K-style math benchmark, the correct diagnosis is often that you are below the emergence threshold, not that the benchmark is too hard or the model is misconfigured. Switching to a 13B or 70B model — or adding chain-of-thought prompting — can move from 10% to 60%+ accuracy with no other changes.
Consider emergence when:

a task requires sequential reasoning steps, all of which must succeed; you observe near-random accuracy on a capable model; you are scaling from a small to a large model and need to set performance expectations.
Do not over-attribute to emergence:

if performance improves smoothly with scale on a continuous metric, the capability is not emergent in the strong sense. Poor zero-shot prompting or evaluation methodology can also produce near-zero performance that falsely resembles sub-threshold behaviour.
Studying Emergence in Practice

Rigorously studying emergence requires evaluating the same model family across multiple scales on the same benchmark, using both binary and continuous metrics. The following code illustrates a framework for plotting emergence curves and detecting apparent phase transitions:

import numpy as np
import json
from pathlib import Path

# Hypothetical benchmark results across model sizes
# In practice, load from evaluation logs (e.g., lm-evaluation-harness output)
results = {
    "gsm8k": {
        # (param_count_billions, exact_match_accuracy, mean_log_prob)
        "points": [
            (0.5,  0.02, -3.21),
            (1.0,  0.03, -3.08),
            (3.0,  0.05, -2.89),
            (7.0,  0.08, -2.74),
            (13.0, 0.17, -2.59),
            (30.0, 0.46, -2.31),
            (70.0, 0.72, -2.09),
            (175.0, 0.83, -1.97),
        ]
    }
}

def analyse_emergence(task_name: str, points: list[tuple]):
    """Compare binary vs continuous metric behaviour across scales."""
    sizes   = [p[0] for p in points]
    binary  = [p[1] for p in points]   # exact-match accuracy
    continu = [p[2] for p in points]   # log-probability (continuous)

    # Slope in log-scale at each size
    log_sizes = np.log10(sizes)
    print(f"\n=== {task_name} — Emergence Analysis ===")
    print(f"{'Size (B)':>10} {'Binary Acc':>12} {'Log-Prob':>12} {'Slope (binary)':>16}")
    for i, (s, b, c) in enumerate(zip(sizes, binary, continu)):
        if i == 0:
            slope = 0.0
        else:
            slope = (b - binary[i-1]) / (log_sizes[i] - log_sizes[i-1] + 1e-9)
        print(f"{s:>10.1f} {b:>12.3f} {c:>12.3f} {slope:>16.3f}")

    # Detect emergence threshold: largest slope region
    slopes = [
        (sizes[i], (binary[i] - binary[i-1]) / (log_sizes[i] - log_sizes[i-1] + 1e-9))
        for i in range(1, len(sizes))
    ]
    threshold_size, max_slope = max(slopes, key=lambda x: x[1])
    print(f"\nPeak slope at ~{threshold_size}B params (slope={max_slope:.2f})")
    print("If log-prob shows smooth improvement, this is a metric artefact, not true emergence.")

for task, data in results.items():
    analyse_emergence(task, data["points"])

# Key insight: compare slope in binary metric vs. continuous metric
# True emergence: BOTH show sharp inflection
# Metric artefact: only binary metric shows inflection; log-prob is smooth
Real-world Example: Chain-of-Thought as Emergence Enabler

Wei et al. (2022) demonstrated that chain-of-thought prompting itself is an emergent capability — it produces no benefit in models below ~100B parameters but large accuracy gains above that threshold. This creates a compounding effect: CoT unlocks reasoning capabilities that are otherwise latent, making the effective emergence threshold conditional on prompting strategy. The following shows how to test this in practice:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

MATH_PROBLEM = """Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of tennis balls.
Each can has 3 balls. How many tennis balls does he have now?"""

def zero_shot(problem: str) -> str:
    """Standard zero-shot: no reasoning steps shown."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=50,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": problem + "\nAnswer:"}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

def chain_of_thought(problem: str) -> str:
    """Chain-of-thought: model explains reasoning step by step."""
    cot_prompt = (
        problem
        + "\nLet's think step by step."
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": cot_prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

print("Zero-shot answer:", zero_shot(MATH_PROBLEM))
print()
print("Chain-of-thought reasoning:")
print(chain_of_thought(MATH_PROBLEM))

# In smaller models (<7B), zero_shot and chain_of_thought produce similar
# (often incorrect) results — CoT capability has not emerged.
# In capable instruction-tuned models, chain_of_thought dramatically
# improves accuracy on multi-step problems by making implicit reasoning explicit.
Common Mistakes When Interpreting Emergence
Evaluating only at one scale.

A single model evaluation cannot distinguish between sub-threshold behaviour and genuine task difficulty. Always evaluate multiple model sizes from the same family on the same benchmark to plot the scaling curve.
Using exact-match accuracy as the sole metric.
Binary metrics are coarse and inflate discontinuities. Supplement with token-level accuracy or log-probability to distinguish genuine emergence from metric non-linearity.
Concluding that a task is impossible for small models without trying CoT.
Chain-of-thought can lower the effective emergence threshold by 5-10x in parameters for many reasoning tasks. Test zero-shot CoT before concluding that a model is below threshold.
Extrapolating safety evaluations linearly.
If a 7B model shows no jailbreak-related capability, this does not imply a 70B model is safe. Dangerous capabilities can emerge at scale without being observable at smaller sizes.
Best Practices for Emergence-Aware Development
Use BIG-Bench Hard (BBH) as a checklist.
BBH contains tasks curated for being hard and near-emergent for most model sizes. Evaluating on BBH gives a rapid signal about whether your model is above or below emergence thresholds for reasoning.
Apply CoT before scaling.

If a task shows near-zero performance on a capable model (>13B), try zero-shot CoT ("Let's think step by step") before spending compute on larger models. CoT can unlock latent capabilities at lower cost than a 10x parameter increase.
Track intermediate checkpoints.

During pretraining, evaluate on a benchmark suite at regular intervals (e.g., every 10% of training tokens). This produces scaling curves that reveal emergence thresholds and allows early termination if target capabilities are reached.
Performance and Scalability

Emergence thresholds in the literature are measured in parameters, but compute (FLOPs) and training tokens are equally important axes. The Chinchilla scaling laws (Hoffmann et al. 2022) showed that a 70B model trained on 1.4T tokens outperforms a 280B model trained on the same compute but 4x fewer tokens. This means emergence thresholds shift depending on the token-to-parameter ratio — a well-trained smaller model can exhibit capabilities usually associated with a larger undertrained model.

Under Chinchilla-optimal training, the effective capability threshold shifts: a 7B model trained on 140B tokens (compute-matched) may exhibit capabilities equivalent to a 13B model trained on 40B tokens. This means that comparing model sizes across labs requires knowing training token counts — parameter count alone is insufficient.
Comparison: Views on Emergence
View
Claim
Evidence
Practical implication
Strong emergence (Wei et al. 2022)
Discontinuous phase transitions at scale
100+ tasks show near-zero then sharp jump
Must scale above threshold for task to work
Metric artefact (Schaeffer et al. 2023)
Smooth improvement; binary metric amplifies
Log-prob curves are smooth across same tasks
Use continuous metrics; gains may be gradual
Multi-component threshold
Each sub-skill scales smoothly; product produces threshold
Mathematical model predicts inflection
Decompose tasks to understand what sub-skills are needed
CoT lowers threshold
Prompting decomposes multi-step tasks
7B + CoT ≈ 70B without CoT on many tasks
Try CoT before scaling to larger model
Security and Reliability Implications

Emergent capabilities create a safety evaluation problem. Red-teaming and capability elicitation performed on a smaller model checkpoint cannot guarantee that a larger checkpoint is equally safe. If deception, advanced persuasion, or jailbreak generalisation emerges above a parameter threshold, this capability is present at deployment even if it was absent during safety review. The Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) and similar frameworks attempt to address this by requiring capability evaluations at each major scale increment, triggering safety measures if certain capability thresholds are crossed.

For practitioners deploying commercial models, the practical implication is that models should be re-evaluated for safety after significant fine-tuning or adapter stacking. Fine-tuning can shift capability distributions and expose or suppress emergent behaviours that were latent in the base model.
Key Takeaways
Emergence describes capabilities that are absent in smaller models and appear abruptly at a scale threshold — not predicted by smooth extrapolation from smaller checkpoints.

The multi-component hypothesis explains emergence: when a task requires k sub-skills each improving smoothly with scale, the joint success probability rises steeply once all components cross individual thresholds simultaneously.

Many observed emergence curves are metric artefacts: binary exact-match amplifies smooth continuous improvements into apparent phase transitions. Always supplement binary metrics with continuous ones (log-probability, per-token accuracy).
Chain-of-thought prompting lowers the effective emergence threshold. A 7B model with CoT can match a 70B model without CoT on many reasoning benchmarks by decomposing multi-step tasks.

Emergence is unpredictable before the fact. There is no reliable method to determine at which scale a specific new capability will appear, which creates challenges for safety evaluation and deployment planning.

Evaluate multiple model sizes from the same family on the same benchmark to distinguish genuine emergence from sub-threshold behaviour. Never conclude a task is impossible based on a single model size evaluation.
Safety-relevant emergent capabilities (deception, jailbreak generalisation) require continuous monitoring across training checkpoints and model versions, not one-time evaluation at a single scale.
